questions.text: What is the full DNS name for a Service 'api' in namespace 'backend'?
questions.explanation: The full DNS name for a Kubernetes Service is <service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local. Within the same namespace, you can use just the service name. From a different namespace, you need at least <service-name>.<namespace>. The cluster domain (cluster.local) is configurable.

questions.text: What Kubernetes resource is required to expose HTTP/HTTPS routes externally via path-based routing?
questions.explanation: Ingress provides HTTP/HTTPS routing with features like path-based routing, hostname-based routing, and TLS termination — all in a single IP. LoadBalancer creates one external IP per service (expensive); Ingress uses one IP/LoadBalancer for many services. Requires an Ingress Controller to function.

questions.text: What does a NetworkPolicy do by default when applied to a Pod?

options.text: Allows all traffic to the Pod
Denies all traffic not explicitly allowed by the policy

questions.explanation: When a NetworkPolicy selects a Pod, all traffic in the specified policyTypes (Ingress/Egress) that is NOT explicitly allowed by the policy is denied. Before any NetworkPolicy applies, all traffic is allowed. NetworkPolicies are additive — multiple policies on a Pod are ORed together.

questions.text: What is a headless Service (clusterIP: None) used for?

options.text: A Service without any backing Pods
Returns individual Pod IPs from DNS; used for StatefulSets and service discovery
A Service that only supports UDP traffic
A Service without a port mapping

questions.explanation: A headless Service (clusterIP: None) doesn't get a ClusterIP. DNS queries return the IPs of individual Pods directly instead of a single virtual IP. This is used with StatefulSets where clients need to connect to specific Pods (db-0, db-1) and for custom load-balancing logic.

questions.text: What does 'pathType: Exact' mean in an Ingress rule?

options.text: Matches only the exact path, not subpaths
Matches the path and all subpaths

questions.explanation: pathType: Exact matches only the exact URL path. /api matches /api but NOT /api/v1. pathType: Prefix matches /api and /api/v1/users. pathType: ImplementationSpecific defers to the Ingress Controller for matching semantics.

questions.text: How does TLS termination work with Kubernetes Ingress?

options.text: TLS is handled by each Pod individually
The Ingress Controller decrypts HTTPS and forwards HTTP to backend Services
TLS certificates are stored in ConfigMaps
Each Service manages its own TLS certificate

questions.explanation: Ingress TLS termination means the Ingress Controller (e.g., nginx-ingress) handles HTTPS on port 443, reads the TLS certificate from a Kubernetes Secret (type: kubernetes.io/tls), and forwards decrypted HTTP traffic to backend Services. This centralizes certificate management.

questions.text: What is an EndpointSlice in Kubernetes 1.35?

options.text: A slice of the Kubernetes API for endpoints
A scalable way to track network endpoints for Services, replacing older Endpoints
A subset of Pods in a Deployment
A partial Service definition for A/B testing

questions.explanation: EndpointSlices (stable in 1.21) replace the older Endpoints API for better scalability. Each EndpointSlice stores up to 100 endpoints (configurable), avoiding large Endpoints objects that caused performance issues in large clusters. kube-proxy uses EndpointSlices by default in 1.21+.

questions.text: Which CNI plugin feature is required for NetworkPolicies to work?

options.text: The CNI plugin must support NetworkPolicy enforcement
kube-proxy must be in iptables mode

questions.explanation: NetworkPolicies require a CNI (Container Network Interface) plugin that supports policy enforcement. Calico, Cilium, WeaveNet, and Antrea support NetworkPolicies. The default kubenet plugin in some managed K8s does NOT enforce NetworkPolicies — they are created but have no effect.

questions.text: What DNS component replaced kube-dns in modern Kubernetes clusters?
questions.explanation: CoreDNS replaced kube-dns as the default DNS server in Kubernetes 1.12. It's a flexible, extensible DNS server written in Go. It runs as a Deployment in the kube-system namespace and uses a Corefile for configuration. Plugins handle Kubernetes service discovery, caching, health checks, and more.

questions.text: What annotation on an Ingress resource specifies which Ingress Controller handles it?
questions.explanation: spec.ingressClassName is the modern approach (Kubernetes 1.18+) to specify which Ingress Controller handles the Ingress. The older kubernetes.io/ingress-class annotation is deprecated but still works. IngressClass resources can be created to register controller names and set a default class.
